finding: §3.3 describes `--quality-gate "<cmd>"` as writing `{"quality_gate": "<cmd>"}` "and nothing else" and `--commit-strategy "<value>"` as writing `{"commit_strategy": "<value>"}` "and nothing else", but the two flags are independent and can be passed together at `tp init`. The per-flag "and nothing else" clauses contradict when both are supplied, and §10.1 only exercises each flag in isolation, so the serialized output of `tp init --quality-gate X --commit-strategy Y` (both fields present, an error, or only one) is unpinned — two engineers write contradictory tests.
suggestion: State the combined case explicitly: `tp init` with both flags writes `{"quality_gate": "<cmd>", "commit_strategy": "<value>"}` (fields composed, no others), and add a §10.1 acceptance row exercising both flags together.

finding: §3.4 defines the `tp config --resolved` `source` label two ways that diverge for a present-but-out-of-range field: (a) 'attributes each workflow field to the layer that supplied it' and (b) 'source: "override" only when the task file explicitly set it'. For a legacy field like `review_clean_rounds: 0` that §7.1/§3.5 preserve as present (explicitly set -> 'override' per criterion b) but clamp at resolution to the built-in default 2 (so the built-in layer supplied the value -> 'default' per criterion a), the two criteria contradict. An implementer cannot tell whether `tp config --resolved` should emit {value, source:"override"} or {value:2, source:"default"}, nor whether the reported `value` is the file's raw value or the clamped effective value.
suggestion: State explicitly what `tp config --resolved` reports for a present-but-clamped (out-of-range) override field: whether `source` follows presence ('override') or the value-supplying layer after clamping ('default'), and whether `value` is the raw file value or the clamped effective value. Add a corresponding assertion to §10.8.

finding: §3.5 (echoed by §7.1/§10.8) says an out-of-range authored/imported override is "treated as unset, falling back to the built-in default." But under the §1/§3.4 precedence (task-override > project config > built-in), an *unset* task override resolves to the NEXT layer — the .tp/config.json project value — not the built-in default. The two clauses therefore name different resolution targets whenever the project layer sets that same field: "treated as unset" implies the project value; "falling back to the built-in default" skips it. §3.5 thus uses the word "unset" with a meaning that contradicts how §1/§3.4 define it. The concrete consequence is that an out-of-range authored value still masks a project-config value (resolves to built-in, bypassing project) — a residual instance of the exact masking bug §1 names as this release's motivation. No §10 test pins this: §10.8 exercises the out-of-range-on-load case with NO project config, so the project-config-present outcome is unspecified.
suggestion: Pick one target and state it unambiguously. If clampWorkflowRanges deliberately clamps to the built-in default regardless of the project layer, drop "treated as unset" (it conflicts with §1's meaning of unset) and write "clamped to the built-in default, bypassing the project layer," flag it as a deliberate exception to the §1 precedence, and add a §10 test with .tp/config.json setting the field to pin that it resolves to built-in (2), not the project value. If instead precedence should be honored, write "treated as absent, resolving through the project layer per §1" and align §7.1/§10.8.

finding: The §10.8 acceptance test asserts an out-of-range authored/imported value "resolves to the built-in default (2) with a warning (§7.1)", but the warning's observable is unpinned: channel (stderr vs stdout vs a JSON `warnings` field), the text/substring to match, and whether the command still exits 0 are all unspecified. The `value: 2` half is deterministic; the "with a warning" half is not verifiable as written, so two engineers assert it differently (or omit the check).
suggestion: Pin the warning observable in §7.1 and reference it from §10.8: specify the stream (e.g. stderr), a stable substring/code to match, and the exit status (e.g. still exit 0).
